description: HARD seed for the semantic-faithfulness tier: subtle misstatements of REAL authorities (overbroad scope, wrong-proposition-right-case, ratio-vs-obiter, superseded), plus hard FAITHFUL controls (accurate but narrow). This is where a model judge is actually stress-tested — unlike the clear-cut validated 8-case set.
labelPolicy: Every `holding` here was AI-seeded from well-known authorities and is marked labelStatus='seed-needs-verification'. Before ANY result from this file is reported as validated, a human MUST verify each holding verbatim against `holdingSource` (the primary source / headnote) and confirm the gold `expectFaithful` label. Do not publish a number from unverified holdings — that would be the very circularity the tier exists to prevent.

difficultyLegend.correct_but_narrow: faithful, but an accurately narrow statement (hard positive)
difficultyLegend.overbroad_scope: real holding stated far more broadly than it holds
difficultyLegend.wrong_proposition_right_case: real authority cited for a point it does not decide
difficultyLegend.ratio_vs_obiter: proposition rests on dicta, not the binding ratio
difficultyLegend.superseded: holding later overruled/qualified; cited as still controlling

cases.holding: The advice to Her Majesty to prorogue Parliament was unlawful because it had the effect of frustrating or preventing, without reasonable justification, the ability of Parliament to carry out its constitutional functions; the prorogation was null and of no effect.
cases.proposition: Miller held that prorogation is unlawful where it frustrates Parliament's constitutional functions without reasonable justification.

cases.holding: The advice to prorogue Parliament was unlawful because it frustrated Parliament's constitutional functions without reasonable justification; the court held the lawfulness of the advice was justiciable.
cases.proposition: Miller establishes that all exercises of the royal prerogative are non-justiciable.

cases.holding: The court imposed Rule 11 sanctions on attorneys who submitted a brief citing non-existent, AI-fabricated cases and failed to withdraw them when their authenticity was questioned.
cases.proposition: Mata v. Avianca sanctioned attorneys for submitting fabricated, AI-generated case citations.

cases.holding: The court imposed Rule 11 sanctions for citing fabricated, AI-generated cases and for the attorneys' lack of candour; it did not hold that using AI research tools is itself sanctionable.
cases.proposition: Mata holds that any use of AI by lawyers is sanctionable misconduct.

cases.holding: The court imposed Rule 11 sanctions on attorneys who submitted a brief citing non-existent, AI-fabricated cases.
cases.proposition: Mata v. Avianca established the standard for airline liability for delayed baggage under the Montreal Convention.

cases.holding: The Fourteenth Amendment requires a State to license a marriage between two people of the same sex and to recognise such a marriage lawfully licensed and performed out-of-State.
cases.proposition: Obergefell held the Fourteenth Amendment requires States to license same-sex marriages.

cases.holding: The Fourteenth Amendment requires States to license and recognise marriages between two people of the same sex.
cases.proposition: Obergefell held that every State must legally recognise all forms of family and domestic partnership.

cases.holding: Separate educational facilities are inherently unequal; racially segregated public schools deprive minority children of the equal protection of the laws under the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.proposition: Brown v. Board held that racially segregated public schools violate the Equal Protection Clause.

cases.holding: In the field of public education the doctrine of 'separate but equal' has no place; segregated public schools violate equal protection. The decision concerned public education.
cases.proposition: Brown held that every racial classification by any arm of government is per se unconstitutional.

cases.holding: Roe v. Wade recognised a constitutional right to obtain an abortion under a trimester framework. It was expressly OVERRULED by Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022), which held the Constitution confers no such right.
cases.proposition: Roe v. Wade, 410 U.S. 113, remains the controlling authority establishing a constitutional right to abortion.

cases.holding: Before custodial interrogation, police must warn a suspect that he has the right to remain silent, that anything he says may be used against him, and that he has the right to counsel; statements obtained without such warnings are generally inadmissible.
cases.proposition: Miranda requires police to warn suspects of their rights before custodial interrogation.

cases.holding: Police must advise a suspect of the right to silence and to counsel before custodial interrogation; the suspect may knowingly and voluntarily waive these rights.
cases.proposition: Miranda requires a defence lawyer to be physically present during all police questioning.

cases.holding: The court criticised submissions that appeared to be artificially constructed using AI and that made up severe allegations without any evidential basis.
cases.proposition: The court criticised AI-constructed submissions for making allegations without an evidential basis.

cases.holding: The court criticised AI-constructed submissions that made baseless allegations; it did not impose any general prohibition on AI in litigation.
cases.proposition: The court in [2025] HKCFI 808 banned the use of AI in Hong Kong litigation.

cases.holding: An advertisement promising a reward to anyone who used the product as directed and still caught influenza was a unilateral offer to the world, accepted by performance of the conditions; the deposit of money showed intention to be bound.
cases.proposition: Carlill held that an advertisement promising a reward can be a unilateral offer accepted by performance.

cases.holding: On its facts the advertisement was an offer (not mere puff) because of the promised deposit; acceptance was by performance. Whether an advertisement is an offer depends on the intention shown.
cases.proposition: Carlill held that every advertisement is a binding contractual offer.
